metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify 3e8b repoints the push-policy citation (concurrency.md L194) to tests/interfaces/store/test_push_policy_e2e.py, not to test_concurrency_regression.py::test_failed_push_never_drops_local_commit
findings.finding: Child 3e8b directs repointing the push-policy citation in docs/concurrency.md (L176/L194) to 'tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py::test_failed_push_never_drops_local_commit', but that test covers WS3 no-data-loss (a push failure must not drop the local commit), not the REBAR_SYNC_PUSH policy modes (always/async/off). The actual Python successor for the deleted tests/scripts/test-rebar-push-policy.sh is tests/interfaces/store/test_push_policy_e2e.py, whose docstring explicitly identifies it as the in-process port of that shell test. If 3e8b lands with the stated repoint, it will cite a test whose invariant does not match the surrounding prose ('Pinned by...' context is the REBAR_SYNC_PUSH policy table).
findings.location: child 3e8b-033c-6cd4-4711, What/files section: 'L176 push-policy citation → repoint to tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py::test_failed_push_never_drops_local_commit'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the 3e8b What/files directive to: 'L194 push-policy citation → repoint to tests/interfaces/store/test_push_policy_e2e.py (the in-process Python port of the deleted shell test that covers the always/async/off REBAR_SYNC_PUSH modes).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify d13e adds an explicit exclude of (os: macos-latest, python-version: '3.13') alongside the new matrix entry, matching the existing macOS single-interpreter cost policy
findings.finding: Child d13e plans to add '3.13' to the Linux python-version matrix without adding a corresponding exclude for (macos-latest, 3.13). The current test.yml excludes only (macos-latest, python-version: '3.11'), so macOS currently runs Python 3.12 only. Adding 3.13 to the shared python-version list without a new exclude entry would cause macOS to run both 3.12 and 3.13, contradicting the stated cost policy ('keep macOS pinned to its single ... entry') and the parent sequencing note ('macOS pinned to its single 3.11 entry'). The child's AC ('test.yml ... Linux matrices include 3.13') does not mention the required macOS exclude.
findings.location: child d13e-e1c3-8bb8-40c4, What/files section and Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: In the d13e What/files section add: also add '- os: macos-latest\n  python-version: "3.13"' to the exclude list in both test.yml and gerrit-verify.yaml to preserve the single-macOS-interpreter policy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in child 5886 which manifest counter (existing `failed_count`, a new `skipped_count`, or reconcile exit-code path) an unmapped-status skip increments, so the 'nonzero exit policy' AC is unambiguously implementable and testable.
findings.finding: Child 5886's AC says unmapped statuses are 'tallied in the pass manifest (nonzero exit policy consistent with other per-mutation failures)' but the plan does not specify *where* in the manifest pipeline the tally increment is placed. The existing manifest shapes count 'applied' and 'failed' (error-keyed outcomes); a skipped-unmapped-status mutation is neither — its placement in the counter hierarchy is unspecified, leaving the executor to guess whether it increments `failed_count`, a new `skipped_count`, or the reconcile exit-code logic.
findings.location: Child 5886 AC: 'tallied in the pass manifest (nonzero exit policy consistent with other per-mutation failures)'; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py:998-1037

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In child c3f8, replace `javascript-typescript` with `javascript` (the canonical `github/codeql-action@v3` language key that covers both JS and TS) in the workflow spec, the 'What / files' narrative, and AC line 1.
findings.finding: Child c3f8 names `javascript-typescript` as the CodeQL language identifier, but `github/codeql-action@v3` uses `javascript` as the canonical key for both JavaScript and TypeScript analysis; `javascript-typescript` is not a valid language name in CodeQL v2+ and will cause the 'Initialize CodeQL' step to fail or silently skip JS/TS analysis.
findings.location: Child ticket c3f8 — 'What / files' section: 'Analyze `python` and `javascript-typescript`'; AC line 1: 'analyzing python + javascript-typescript'
findings.suggested_fix: In the `language` matrix of `.github/workflows/codeql.yml`, use `language: [python, javascript]`. Update the c3f8 description and AC line 1 to read 'analyzing python + javascript' accordingly. The `javascript` key in CodeQL v2+/v3 automatically detects and analyzes both `.js` and `.ts` source files.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan (or in child 5886's AC) that existing Jira status-map configs were audited (or will be validated on startup) to confirm no in-use status is currently unmapped, bounding the blast radius of the skip-on-unknown behavior change.
findings.finding: The unmapped-status guard (child 5886) inverts observable behavior for all inbound Jira syncs — from 'silently reopen' to 'skip + alert' — but the plan does not acknowledge whether any downstream consumer or workflow configuration depends on the old fall-through behavior, nor confirm that a sweep for such dependents was done.
findings.location: Context — 'unmapped-status guard (child 5886)'; Success Criteria — 'Inbound Jira statuses that have no mapping can no longer silently reopen local tickets (skipped + alerted + tallied)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line AC to child 5886: 'A startup/config-load warning (or fsck check) is emitted if any status observed in a recent sync window is absent from the mapping, so operators learn of gaps before the next sync skips transitions.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before child 02cd is implemented, verify and document in the child body the specific pydantic-ai 1.x API surface that enforces a wall-clock HTTP timeout (e.g. `AnthropicProvider(http_client=httpx.Client(timeout=N))`), and name the SDK's own effective default timeout value so the 'no lower than SDK default' floor is a concrete, testable number.
findings.finding: Child 02cd asserts that `timeout_s` / `REBAR_LLM_TIMEOUT` will 'demonstrably bound LLM calls' via the pydantic-ai SDK, but the plan leaves the specific API surface unresolved ('02cd selects the surface for the installed pydantic-ai version') without first verifying that such a surface exists and works as expected. Code inspection confirms `timeout_s` is resolved in config.py (line 519) but is passed to NO argument on any of the three `agent.run_sync()` call sites (runner.py lines 318, 456, 469). pydantic-ai 1.x exposes per-call HTTP timeout via the provider's httpx client (e.g. `AnthropicProvider(http_client=httpx.Client(timeout=N))`) or model-level settings — NOT a `run_sync` kwarg named `timeout` — making the wiring mechanism a non-trivial API discovery task. Additionally, the 'no lower than SDK default' floor constraint in both the epic AC and child-02cd body is unverifiable because neither the plan nor the codebase names the pydantic-ai/httpx SDK default timeout value (httpx default: 5s connect + 5s read, far below the 600s `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_S`).
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'The operator-configured LLM timeout demonstrably bounds LLM calls'; child 02cd description 'rebar's default must be at least the SDK's default'; src/rebar/llm/runner.py lines 318, 456, 469; src/rebar/llm/config.py line 519
findings.suggested_fix: In child 02cd, add: 'The wiring surface for pydantic-ai >=1,<2 is `AnthropicProvider(http_client=httpx.Client(timeout=httpx.Timeout(timeout_s)))` passed to `AnthropicModel(..., provider=provider)` — verified against pydantic-ai 1.x source (pydantic_ai/providers/anthropic.py). The httpx default timeout is 5s connect / 5s read; since DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_S=600 >> 5s, the "no lower than SDK default" constraint means: when timeout_s is unset, pass nothing (let httpx use its default) rather than passing 600 as a hardcoded override. The test must mock httpx.Client construction and assert timeout=600 when REBAR_LLM_TIMEOUT=600, and timeout=httpx.USE_CLIENT_DEFAULT (or None) when unset.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge child 5886 as a behavioral inversion (not purely additive), clarify that `git revert` addresses new occurrences but not tickets already skipped during the window, and add a one-sentence migration note (e.g., 'operators should audit Jira status mappings before landing; any status gap will produce alerts from first run').
findings.finding: Child 5886 (unmapped-status guard) is a behavioral inversion — unmapped Jira statuses previously silently defaulted to `open`; after the change they are skipped and alerted. The plan's rollout note characterises ALL runtime changes as 'additive or hardens an existing best-effort path', which is inaccurate here. Integrations that depended (even inadvertently) on the silent-default will change behavior in production. The generic per-change `git revert` rollback is stated but the plan does not acknowledge 5886 as the one exception to the 'additive' claim, nor does it confirm `git revert` is sufficient (e.g., whether any already-processed tickets will be left in an incorrect state after revert).
findings.location: Sequencing & rollout note; child 5886 bullet in Context and Success Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: In the Sequencing & rollout note, add a parenthetical: '(5886 is the sole exception: it is a behaviour inversion for the unmapped-status path; operators should verify their Jira status-mapping config before landing this change, as `git revert` will not retroactively re-process tickets skipped during the deployment window)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add positive rationale to the e699 plan (and update the push_after_commit docstring) clarifying that acquiring the write lock inside the merge sub-path of push_tickets_branch is safe because push_after_commit is always called after the write lock has been released, and update the docstring to reflect that push_tickets_branch now acquires the lock internally for its merge step.
findings.finding: The e699 push-merge lock approach acquires the write lock inside `push_tickets_branch`, but the calling function `push_after_commit` (push.py:245) has an explicit docstring contract stating 'must not nest inside the write lock.' The plan does not provide positive rationale explaining why locking inside the push path is now safe (it is safe because `push_after_commit` is always called after the lock is released, but this reasoning is absent). Without this rationale, the implementer may misread the existing docstring as a blocker, and the docstring itself becomes misleading after the change — creating a maintenance trap where future callers might avoid calling `push_after_commit` under a lock context that has already been released.
findings.location: e699 child story — 'What' section / push.py:245 docstring contract
findings.suggested_fix: In the e699 'What' section, add: 'This is safe because push_after_commit (and all callers of push_tickets_branch) run only after the write lock has been released — there is no nesting. Update push_after_commit's docstring to clarify that push_tickets_branch now internally acquires the write lock around its merge step, so callers must not hold the lock when invoking it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Sharpen the 0e59 AC to explicitly state the required ordering: `git add` → check return code → `git commit` only if add succeeded (i.e., commit must not run when add failed), not merely that a check exists somewhere before commit.
findings.finding: The 0e59 plan's AC says 'git add's return code is checked before git commit runs', but the current event_append.py code (lines 132-136) unconditionally runs `git commit` before the `if add.returncode != 0` check at line 137. This means a failed `git add` followed by a successful `git commit` silently commits whatever was pre-staged (exactly the phantom-event bug). The plan correctly identifies the fix, but the parent epic's description ('the git-add return code is also checked before commit') understates the mechanism: it is not merely an 'also' check — commit must be conditional on add success. The AC should explicitly state that the reordering must be `add → check rc → commit (only if add succeeded)` to avoid an implementation that adds the check as a guard but still runs commit unconditionally.
findings.location: 0e59 AC — 'git add's return code is checked before git commit runs' / event_append.py:127-150
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC line to: 'git add's return code is checked before git commit is invoked: commit must not run when add has failed (not merely checked after-the-fact).' This disambiguates reordering from a post-hoc guard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend child 5886's scope to also fix `inbound_translate._jira_status_to_local` (and its callers in `apply_inbound_records.py`) with the same skip+alert rule, so unmapped statuses on the inbound *create* path are also surfaced rather than defaulting to 'open'.
findings.finding: Child 5886's skip+alert decision rule targets only `inbound_differ.py` but an identical `"open"` fallback for unmapped Jira statuses exists in a second, independent code path — `inbound_translate._jira_status_to_local` (line 146), called by `apply_inbound_records.py` at lines 133 and 285 for the inbound *create* path. The plan's fix specification is not co-located with this second path, so newly-created local tickets from unmapped-status Jira issues would still silently default to 'open'.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py` to child 5886's `file_impact` and update `_jira_status_to_local` to return `None` (or raise a sentinel) for unmapped statuses, letting callers (`apply_inbound_records.py`) emit the bridge alert and skip the status write — mirroring the same protocol specified for `inbound_differ.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend child 5886's scope to also guard the unmapped-status fallback in inbound_translate.py::_jira_status_to_local() (called by apply_inbound_records.py lines 133 and 285), or create a follow-on ticket explicitly tracking this remainder
findings.finding: Child 5886's scope targets only inbound_differ.py:224-225, but the identical unmapped-status-to-'open' fallback also exists in inbound_translate.py::_jira_status_to_local() (line 146: 'return mapping.get(jira_status, "open")'), which is called by apply_inbound_records.py at lines 133 (inbound CREATE status resolution) and 285 (inbound STATUS-update resolution). No sibling child covers this second call path. The parent success criterion ('inbound Jira statuses that have no mapping can no longer silently reopen local tickets') covers all inbound paths, not only the inbound differ path.
findings.location: child 5886-d028-7954-4763, Scope section ('In: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_differ.py failure path') and Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) expand 5886's scope to also fix _jira_status_to_local() in inbound_translate.py to skip+alert instead of returning 'open', or (b) add a sibling child for the apply_inbound_records.py path and make it explicit in the parent AC that 5886 covers only the differ path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a dep from 3003 to 3e8b (or note in 3003's AC) that its concurrency.md additions must not re-introduce the dead citations or CI claims deleted by 3e8b
findings.finding: Child 3e8b has an explicit ordering dependency on child 3003 that is undeclared: both modify docs/concurrency.md, 3e8b lands first in the Gerrit stack, and 3003's AC includes 'docs/concurrency.md updated to describe the surfacing behavior'. If 3003's implementation re-introduces content near the citations that 3e8b deletes (e.g. by adding new test citations in the same paragraph regions), a rebase conflict or silent contradiction could arise. Neither child declares a dep on the other and neither child's AC explicitly says 'do not re-introduce the deleted citations'.
findings.location: child 3003-774d-23dc-4697 AC ('docs/concurrency.md updated to describe the surfacing behavior') and child 3e8b-033c-6cd4-4711 What/files (edits to docs/concurrency.md)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 3003's AC: 'docs/concurrency.md additions must not re-introduce the dead shell-test citations (tests/scripts/*.sh) or the REBAR_FORCE_MKDIR_LOCK CI-hook claim removed by 3e8b.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the CodeQL AC into two explicit parts: an in-session close condition ('workflow file present and passes yaml-lint / zizmor in CI') and a separate post-landing follow-up ('first-run findings triaged in <linked issue>'), so the story can be closed at merge time.
findings.finding: The AC line for CodeQL (c3f8) bundles an in-session deliverable ('file valid & landed') with an explicitly post-landing process item ('first-run triage tracked post-landing') inside a single bullet, making it impossible to determine when the criterion is fully satisfied in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — OSS/CI children bullet: 'CodeQL workflow c3f8 — file valid & landed, first-run triage tracked post-landing'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the bullet as: 'CodeQL workflow c3f8 — **in-session close condition**: workflow file valid, committed, and CI green; **post-landing follow-up** (tracked in <issue/slug>): first-run findings triaged within N days of first mirror run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'mypy ratchet start' in the AC with a specific observable outcome for d2fa, e.g., 'mypy runs in CI on [module/package] under [named flag set] with zero errors, enforced by a CI step that fails on new errors.'
findings.finding: The AC for the mypy child (d2fa) uses effort-framing ('mypy strictness ratchet started') rather than stating an observable outcome, leaving an executor unable to determine when the criterion is satisfied.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Maintainability children bullet: 'mypy ratchet start d2fa'
findings.suggested_fix: State the minimum observable bar in the epic AC: e.g., 'mypy runs on src/ with --strict (or a named subset of flags) in CI, zero errors at merge, fail-on-error enforced — further strictness expansion tracked in d2fa.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the Context deliverable description for child 5886 to include 'tallied' (e.g. change 'skipped + alerted' to 'skipped + alerted + tallied') so it matches the Success Criteria and AC wording.
findings.finding: The Context deliverable description for child 5886 says 'skipped + alerted' but the Success Criteria and AC both add 'tallied' (e.g. 'skipped + alerted + tallied') — the tally requirement is present in the verifiable gates but absent from the narrative description of 5886, creating a terminology gap that could cause the implementer to miss the tally obligation.
findings.location: Context (child 5886 bullet) vs. Success Criteria (unmapped-status bullet) vs. AC line 'Inbound Jira statuses that have no mapping can no longer silently reopen local tickets (skipped + alerted + tallied)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Context section, update the 5886 bullet to: 'an inbound Jira status with no mapping is skipped + alerted + tallied (counter incremented) instead of silently defaulting to open.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation target (e.g., CONTRIBUTING.md section or a docs/dev-guide page) to children d2fa and 0f21 covering (a) how to register a new env var and (b) the mypy annotation scope required to pass the ratchet.
findings.finding: The new mypy strictness ratchet (d2fa) and the env-var registry + CI drift gate (0f21) are new contributor-facing requirements — they will cause CI failures for contributors who don't know the rules — but the plan names no documentation target (CONTRIBUTING.md, docs/dev-guide, inline pyproject.toml comment, etc.) where contributors will learn about them.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Maintainability children (mypy ratchet start d2fa, env-var registry + drift gate 0f21) landed'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC for d2fa, add: 'CONTRIBUTING.md updated with mypy strictness scope and how to run the check locally.' In the AC for 0f21, add: 'CONTRIBUTING.md (or docs/env-vars.md) updated with instructions for registering a new env var to pass the drift gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md section (or ADR) documenting the env-var registry pattern introduced by 0f21: what the registry is, where it lives, and how contributors add a new entry — and reference it from the drift-gate CI error message or a CONTRIBUTING note.
findings.finding: The env-var registry + drift gate (child 0f21) introduces a new cross-cutting architectural pattern: all future operator-visible config must be registered in a central registry or CI will fail. No ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or contributing-guide section is called out anywhere in the plan to document this contract for future contributors.
findings.location: AC: 'Maintainability children (mypy ratchet start d2fa, env-var registry + drift gate 0f21) landed'; Sequencing note omits any docs artifact for 0f21
findings.suggested_fix: In child 0f21's scope, add a CLAUDE.md paragraph under a 'Configuration' or 'Operator Config' heading that names the registry file/function, explains the CI drift gate, and gives a one-line example of registering a new env var. Reference this from the drift-gate CI step's failure output (e.g., `echo 'See CLAUDE.md §Configuration to register a new env var'`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an epic-level observable proof for the mypy ratchet: specify that d2fa must also wire a `mypy` CI step (or extend test.yml) so the ratchet is enforced in CI, not just configured — matching the 'CI drift gate' proof already stated for the env-var registry goal.
findings.finding: The mypy ratchet success criterion ('ratchet started') and its AC ('Maintainability children … d2fa … landed') specify only that the child lands, not that mypy runs in CI with a gate — leaving it possible to close d2fa by adding a pyproject.toml stanza without any CI enforcement, which does not constitute a 'ratchet in place.'
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Typing and config-surface ratchets are in place (mypy strictness ratchet started; env-var registry with a CI drift gate)'; AC line 'Maintainability children (mypy ratchet start d2fa, env-var registry + drift gate 0f21) landed'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the Success Criteria to read 'mypy strictness ratchet started and gated in CI (mypy step added to test.yml, initially at baseline error count or --ignore-missing-imports)' and add a parallel AC line: 'mypy runs in CI with a non-zero-findings gate at the configured strictness level.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether the four concern categories (reliability seams, maintainability ratchets, docs drift, OSS/CI hygiene) should be deliverable as independent sub-stacks or waves rather than a single 13-change ordered stack, so that a failing hygiene change cannot delay landing of high-urgency reliability fixes.
findings.finding: The epic bundles four genuinely distinct concern categories (reliability seams, maintainability ratchets, docs drift, OSS/CI hygiene) with different actors/personas and independent release value into a single ordered Gerrit stack. While children are present and individually scoped, the stack coupling means a low-urgency hygiene child (e.g. README badges, .gitignore) could delay or force revert of high-urgency reliability children, and the four concern areas have different 'reasons to change' that may evolve at different cadences.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
